data.body:
DESIGN-OF-RECORD — asymmetric-signing + authenticated-identity, restructured into THREE epics.

GOALS: (A) auditability — trustworthy "who/what performed this rebar operation"; (B) provider-neutral assignee mapping across the ticket systems rebar syncs with (Jira now; GitHub Issues / Linear later — architecture must not over-fit Jira).

THREE-EPIC STRUCTURE (dependency graph): Epic 1 (foundation) is a prerequisite for BOTH Epic 2 and Epic 3, which are parallel siblings.
- Epic 1 — Asymmetric attestation FOUNDATION (identity-free). DSSE envelope + PAE; SSHSIG scheme via OpenSSH ssh-keygen (no new core dep; stdlib subprocess; fail-closed); pluggable scheme registry + per-kind policy table {kind -> scheme, namespace, trust-root}; register existing HMAC as a LEGACY scheme (behavior unchanged).
- Epic 2 — Authenticated identity: entity + AUTHORSHIP certification (PERSON actor) + provider-neutral user mapping. Reuses Epic 1. git-bug entity model on rebar events; opaque rebar id (NOT self-certifying — the key gives tamper-evidence); attribution always (name+email) + optional authorship signature (namespace rebar.authorship.v1) verified vs identity in-band keys (TOFU genesis + G2 signed rotation). Provider-neutral {provider, external_id} keyed on the provider's opaque id (never email); Jira first adapter; placeholder + idempotent auto-upgrade for unmapped inbound. Deliberate creation. Enforcement opt-in, enforced at the MERGE gate (local write-gate is UX-only); replay never rejects.
- Epic 3 — Operation certificates: asymmetric + ENVIRONMENT-attributed (ENVIRONMENT actor) + per-project optional required-environment. Reuses Epic 1; INDEPENDENT of Epic 2. Any environment signs (Ed25519, namespace rebar.opcert.v1); verify-by-anyone against the env's public key. Low-security default: local certificate = process record. High-security: project pins a trusted-environment public key OUT-OF-BAND and the merge gate requires that environment's certificate; developers obtain it by routing the gate to a trusted rebar-mcp server (e.g. AWS). Removes the legacy HMAC scheme (contract phase). END STATE: asymmetric for BOTH signature types.

KEY DECISIONS: adopt established standards over custom crypto — DSSE (envelope, PAE defeats JSON-canonicalization + JWT alg-confusion), OpenSSH SSHSIG (reference impl; reuse dev SSH keys; verified fail-closed experimentally), git's allowed_signers trust-file model. securesystemslib rejected (no SSH signer; pulls cryptography). Two attestation kinds stay ORTHOGONAL (different actor, threat model, key, trust-root); they SHARE only non-security plumbing (record/verify/storage) via a per-kind pluggable scheme; a signature is never valid across kinds (namespace + signed kind domain-separate them).

SECURITY CONSTRAINTS (must hold; from adversarial review vs RFC 8725 / DSSE / OpenSSH / real CVEs):
- Never let a record select its own scheme/algorithm; pin scheme+key-source per kind out-of-band (anti alg-confusion).
- Sign exact stored bytes via DSSE PAE; never re-serialize parsed JSON at verify.
- High-authority roots (op-cert trusted-env keys) live OUT-OF-BAND (Gerrit-protected / CI), never the auto-pushed tickets branch. Authorship keys may be in-band + TOFU (low authority).
- Enforcement lives at the MERGE gate, not the local write-gate (local is bypassable/UX-only).
- "Require environment E" verifies against E's pinned public key, NOT the certificate's self-claimed key_id (DSSE keyid is an unauthenticated hint).
- Key validity scoped to merged-log position (era), not attacker-controllable wall-clock event timestamps.
- Op-cert INTEGRITY (high-security) requires the trusted environment to fetch AUTHORITATIVE state itself (ticket state + code-at-commit) and run the gate on that — never client-provided inputs.
- Fail-closed subprocess contract for ssh-keygen: argv arrays, allowlist-validate namespace/principal, exit-code only, detect >=8.9.
